name: Furnariidae
description: Furnariidae is a large family of Neotropical passerines that includes ovenbirds, woodcreepers, and foliage-gleaners. They build distinctive mud or stick nests, many resembling small ovens.

children.name: Furnarius
children.description: Furnarius is a genus of ovenbirds named for their distinctive oven-shaped mud nests, found across much of South America.

children.name: Furnarius rufus
children.description: The rufous hornero is the national bird of Argentina and Uruguay, famous for its intricate mud nest shaped like a dome oven. It is a common sight across open habitats of eastern South America.

children.name: Furnarius cristatus
children.description: The crested hornero is a small hornero with a distinctive crest, found in dry woodlands and scrub of the Gran Chaco region in Bolivia, Paraguay, and Argentina.

children.name: Furnarius figulus
children.description: The band-tailed hornero is named for the distinctive dark band across its tail, found near water in eastern Brazil. It builds mud nests on branches overhanging water.

children.name: Furnarius leucopus
children.description: The pale-legged hornero is a medium-sized ovenbird with pale pinkish legs, found in lowland forests and woodlands of Amazonia and the Guianas.

children.name: Cinclodes
children.description: Cinclodes are terrestrial ovenbirds adapted to cold, rocky environments along the Andes and Patagonia.

children.name: Cinclodes fuscus
children.description: The bar-winged cinclodes is an Andean songbird that inhabits grassy slopes and streamsides from Venezuela to Tierra del Fuego. It is named for the distinctive white wing bar visible in flight.

children.name: Pygarrhichas
children.description: Pygarrhichas is a monotypic genus of ovenbird with a unique woodcreeper-like foraging style, spiraling up tree trunks.

children.name: Pygarrhichas albogularis
children.description: The white-throated treerunner is a small, rufous-and-white bird of southern South American forests. It climbs tree trunks like a woodcreeper, using its stiff tail for support.

children.name: Lepidocolaptes
children.description: Lepidocolaptes is a genus of slender, arboreal woodcreepers with narrow, slightly decurved bills.

children.name: Lepidocolaptes angustirostris
children.description: The narrow-billed woodcreeper is a widespread bird of open woodlands and savannas across southern South America. It uses its slender bill to extract insects from bark crevices.

children.name: Xiphocolaptes
children.description: Xiphocolaptes is a genus of large, powerful woodcreepers with strong bills found in Central and South American forests.

children.name: Xiphocolaptes major
children.description: The great rufous woodcreeper is one of the largest woodcreepers, with rich rufous plumage and a robust, slightly decurved bill. It inhabits lowland forests of central South America.

children.name: Sittasomus
children.description: Sittasomus is a monotypic genus of small, slender woodcreeper with olive-brown plumage.

children.name: Sittasomus griseicapillus
children.description: The olivaceous woodcreeper is a small, drab woodcreeper found from Mexico to Argentina. It is the most widespread woodcreeper species, occupying a vast range of forest types.

children.name: Anabacerthia
children.description: Anabacerthia is a genus of foliage-gleaners that forage by searching dead leaves for arthropods in the forest understory.

children.name: Anabacerthia amaurotis
children.description: The white-eyed foliage-gleaner is a small, brownish ovenbird with a distinctive pale eye, endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil. It gleans insects from dead leaves in the understory.

children.name: Aphrastura
children.description: Aphrastura is a genus of small, active ovenbirds with distinctive thorn-like tail feathers, endemic to southern South America.

children.name: Aphrastura spinicauda
children.description: The thorn-tailed rayadito is a small,活泼 bird of southern beech forests in Chile and Argentina. Its tail feathers end in sharp bare shafts, giving the species its common name.

children.name: Heliobletus
children.description: Heliobletus is a monotypic genus of small treehunter with a sharply pointed bill, endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil.

children.name: Heliobletus contaminatus
children.description: The sharp-billed treehunter is a small, olive-brown bird with a fine pointed bill, found in montane forests of southeastern Brazil and adjacent Argentina and Paraguay.

children.name: Dendrocolaptes
children.description: Dendrocolaptes is a genus of medium to large woodcreepers with heavy, slightly decurved bills, found in forests from Mexico to Argentina.

children.name: Dendrocolaptes picumnus
children.description: The black-banded woodcreeper is a heavily barred woodcreeper with a robust bill, found in lowland and montane forests across much of South America.

children.name: Dendrocolaptes certhia
children.description: The Amazonian barred woodcreeper is a large, heavily barred species found throughout the Amazon Basin, where it favors humid lowland forests.

children.name: Hylexetastes
children.description: Hylexetastes is a genus of large woodcreepers with distinctive reddish bills, found in Amazonian rainforests.

children.name: Hylexetastes perrotii
children.description: The red-billed woodcreeper is a large, robust woodcreeper with a striking reddish-orange bill, found in the canopy of lowland Amazonian forests.

children.name: Dendrocincla
children.description: Dendrocincla is a genus of small to medium woodcreepers with relatively short, wedge-shaped bills, found in forests from Mexico to Argentina.

children.name: Dendrocincla merula
children.description: The white-chinned woodcreeper is a drab brown woodcreeper with a distinctive white chin patch, found in Amazonian lowland forests where it often follows army ant swarms.

children.name: Dendrocincla turdina
children.description: The plain-winged woodcreeper is a thrush-like woodcreeper of the Atlantic Forest, lacking the wing markings typical of most woodcreepers.

children.name: Xiphorhynchus
children.description: Xiphorhynchus is a diverse genus of medium to large woodcreepers with long, decurved bills, found in forests from Mexico to Argentina.

children.name: Xiphorhynchus erythropygius
children.description: The spotted woodcreeper is a richly spotted woodcreeper with a long decurved bill, found in humid forests from southern Mexico to Ecuador.

children.name: Xiphorhynchus guttatus
children.description: The buff-throated woodcreeper is a large, streaked woodcreeper with a buffy throat and breast, widespread across Amazonian and Guianan forests.

children.name: Xiphorhynchus susurrans
children.description: The cocoa woodcreeper is a medium-sized woodcreeper found from Honduras through northern South America, with rufous-brown plumage and a long decurved bill.

children.name: Dendroplex
children.description: Dendroplex is a genus of woodcreepers with relatively straight bills, found in lowland forests and woodlands of South America.

children.name: Dendroplex picus
children.description: The straight-billed woodcreeper is a widespread species with a distinctive straight bill, found in a variety of wooded habitats from Panama to Argentina.

children.name: Campylorhamphus
children.description: Campylorhamphus is a genus of woodcreepers with spectacularly long, sickle-shaped bills, found in forests from Panama to Argentina.

children.name: Campylorhamphus procurvoides
children.description: The curve-billed scythebill is a small scythebill with a remarkably long, decurved bill, found in Amazonian lowland forests where it probes dead leaves and bark crevices.

children.name: Xenops
children.description: Xenops is a genus of small, active ovenbirds with a distinctive wedge-shaped bill, found in forests from Mexico to Argentina.

children.name: Xenops rutilans
children.description: The streaked xenops is a small, streaky brown bird with a pale eyebrow and a distinctive upturned wedge bill, found in montane and lowland forests across much of South America.

children.name: Xenops minutus
children.description: The plain xenops is a small, drab brown bird with a pale stripe above the eye, found from southern Mexico to Amazonia. It forages gleaning insects from dead leaves and bark.

children.name: Cranioleuca
children.description: Cranioleuca is a genus of small, active spinetails with rufous crowns and long tails, found in forests and woodlands of Central and South America.

children.name: Cranioleuca vulpina
children.description: The rusty-backed spinetail has a rich rufous back and crown with pale underparts, found in riparian thickets and gallery forests across much of central South America.

children.name: Synallaxis
children.description: Synallaxis is the largest genus of spinetails, with many species found in a variety of habitats from Mexico to Argentina. They build large, domed stick nests.

children.name: Synallaxis ruficapilla
children.description: The rufous-capped spinetail has a distinctive rufous cap and wing patches, endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, where it skulks in dense undergrowth.

children.name: Synallaxis spixi
children.description: Spix's spinetail is a small, brown spinetail with a rufous crown and long pointed tail, found in scrubby habitats and forest edges of southeastern South America.

children.name: Synallaxis albescens
children.description: The pale-breasted spinetail is a small, pale spinetail with a rufous cap and whitish underparts, found in open grassy and scrubby habitats from Costa Rica to Argentina.

children.name: Synallaxis frontalis
children.description: The sooty-fronted spinetail has a dark grey forehead and face with rufous crown and wings, found in dry woodlands and scrub across much of southern South America.
